What's funny is that with the current approach, we don't expose the functions or e.g. the `sys` module because of dropped globals:

	>>> import Qt
	>>> dir(Qt)
	['Enginio', 'Qt', 'QtBluetooth', 'QtCore', 'QtDesigner', 'QtGui', 'QtHelp', 'QtLocation', 'QtMacExtras', 'QtMultimedia', 'QtMultimediaWidgets', 'QtNetwork', 'QtNfc', 'QtOpenGL', 'QtPositioning', 'QtPrintSupport', 'QtQml', 'QtQuick', 'QtQuickWidgets', 'QtSensors', 'QtSerialPort', 'QtSql', 'QtSvg', 'QtWebChannel', 'QtWebEngineWidgets', 'QtWebSockets', 'QtWidgets', 'QtXml', 'QtXmlPatterns', '__binding__', '__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__']

If we were to "fix" that, we'd instead see all those functions etc:

	>>> import Qt
	>>> dir(Qt)
	['PyQt5', '__builtins__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__name__', '__package__', '_load_pyqt4', '_load_pyqt5', '_load_pyside', '_load_pyside2', 'sys']

So, in a way, it could be quite useful to clean house and do the `sys.modules['Qt']` thing after all. But perhaps we should do that as the very last thing, rather than doing it in the middle of a function.

It feels like a hack but in this case when we're making a wrapper, it makes sense.

In the newest version in master, `Affine` now has a `backend` parameter, which can be set to `cv2` (OpenCV), `skimage` (scikit-image) or `auto` (chooses `cv2` wherever inputs allow it). The parameter defaults to `auto`.

OpenCV will be used in `auto` mode if the image dtype is `uint8`, there are <=4 image channels and order is among `[0, 1, 3]`. In `cv2` mode, order may also be 2 or 4, then these orders will automatically be mapped to 3 (`cv2.INTER_CUBIC`).

According to my tests, functionality seems to be essentially the same as in the scikit-image version. OpenCV supports using three values as cval though (i.e. full RGB colors instead of just grayscale). Will have to add this later as it caused problems today.

Not sure if PIL is added at any point. It doesn't seem to provide much additional value over the opencv/scikit-image combination.

I like the idea of conforming to the abstract base class MutableMapping. The problem is, lrucache doesn't actually conform. PEP 3119 states that Mappings should have keys(), values(), etc. return something that conforms to Set. But, lrucache returns iterators, which don't. Side note, the decision to return iterators was a design mistake I made in the early days of pylru.

If there is specific functionality from the dict interface, that you want in lrucache, I think the easiest thing would be to implement it directly, at least for now. I just pushed some changes to master that may help you.

If you want lrucache to conform to MutableMapping, that requires changing the API and I don't want to break code for current users that expect to get an iterator. We could change the API in a new 2.0 release and list both on PyPI. I'm not sure if there is enough demand to justify that or not.

Not so many samples. If we consider like 7bytes each for 'invalid', it is 4.1Mb for the channel. If you have many channels, could grow but it does not seem to be too big.

> will the integers still get resampled?
As it stands, no, they won't. In general i found that integer signals should not be interpolated, because they contain bit encoded data in most cases. As such, the value between 43 and 59, for example, should not be 51. Channels that make sense when being interpolated are usually floats.

In order to introduce flexibility, some sort of channel filter list would have to be introduced to tell the interpolate function to treat that channel differently. That would involve a substantial change.
Another thing you could do before resampling a file is this:
```
intChannelsForInterp = ['channel1', 'channel2'....]
```

The difference is when you cause a KeyboardInterrupt in the **main** thread vs. in the **child** thread. The main thread is waiting for all these child processes to wrap up their work, so when you interrupt the main thread it can exit like right away. But when you throw an exception in a child process, the main thread needs to get to that child. So it waits for the first child's result, then the second's etc until it tries to wait on the child that threw an exception. That child's result has probably been ready for a while, but it hasn't been waited on until now. At this moment the exception gets thrown in the main thread.

Making it so that the main thread handles the child results as they come back is kind of complicated I think and probably not something I have time to address in the near future.

The change that happened here is that in 0.13.x, SSLyze will try all possible ciphers and ssl versions at the very beginning, in order to find which ones work with the server. There are a couple of things going on there.

1. The reason ```visualize_cam``` output is bad because the penultimate conv layer have an output shape of (1, 1, 128). So we are essentially only getting (1, 1) output for weights. Try using the conv layer before that which has (5, 5) image resolution. You can do so by setting ```penultimate_layer_idx=3 or 2```. By default, it picks the lowest conv layer which has too small of an output resolution.

2. Even after I set a higher penultimate layer, the output was a constant heatmap, but that was because of a numerical issue. Essentially with small grad values, the computation is overflowing float32 precision and turning into zeros, effectively wiping out the heatmap information. since heatmap is initialized with all 1's you are seeing that red output everywhere. I just pushed a commit to fix that.

3. K.set_learning_phase(0) on the top, is a keras weirdness. Keras maintains an internal dictionary of various learning phases in a model graph (for example dropout) during construction time. By setting it ahead of model construction, you are also messing up your own model. to see what i mean, set a beakpoint of k.learning_phase() within keras code to see how it gets triggered with and without that top line. I noticed that it doesnt register learning phase for second dropout which is odd. I will investigate further but it might be a keras bug. In either case, this is more of a keras issue and i am out of options on what i can do here. There might be some hacky workarounds but i wouldn't want to include it in the lib.

Ah, that's actually my code that Sanjay dug up on ActiveState before I'd even heard of Eppy. The solution is, in eppy.geometry.surface.py, to explicitly test for the case where the points are all in line. The easiest way to do this is to test after the for loop in `area(poly)`. It's also more efficient to test after the calculation rather than test for colinearity beforehand, given that it's probably rare that the function will be passed vertices that are in a straight line.

```python
def area(poly):
    """Area of a polygon poly""" 
    if len(poly) < 3: # not a plane - no area
        return 0
    total = [0, 0, 0]
    N = len(poly)
    for i in range(N):
        vi1 = poly[i]
        vi2 = poly[(i+1) % N]
        prod = np.cross(vi1, vi2)
        total[0] += prod[0]
        total[1] += prod[1]
        total[2] += prod[2]
    if total == [0, 0, 0]:  # points are in a straight line
        return 0
    result = np.dot(total, unit_normal(poly[0], poly[1], poly[2]))
    return abs(result/2)
```

> I am new to bacnet and bacpypes. I am attempting to connect a Shark

By "objects" you mean devices, correct?  In BACnet terms the "object" is 
the analog or binary inputs and outputs.

According to the Shark documentation, the default BACnet Device Number 
is generated from the MAC address, so the Who-Is request you send out 
has to (a) not have any low or high limit or (b) have the low and high 
limit encompass the number that is on the BACnet/IP Interface 
configuration web page.  From the WhoIsIAm.py application it should be 
as simple as this:

     > whois *:*

Which is a global broadcast.  If you run it with a debugger attached to 
the UDP module like this:

     $ python WhoIsIAm.py --debug bacpypes.udp

Then you should see the Who-Is going out on the broadcast IP address for 
your workstation.  For example, my address is 10.0.1.205/24 so the 
broadcast is sent to the ('10.1.0.255', 47808) socket address:

     DEBUG:bacpypes.udp.UDPActor:indication <bacpypes.pdu.PDU object at 
0x101399f10>
         <bacpypes.pdu.PDU object at 0x101399f10>
             pduDestination = ('10.0.1.255', 47808)
             pduExpectingReply = 0

     DEBUG:bacpypes.udp.UDPDirector:handle_write
     DEBUG:bacpypes.udp.UDPDirector:    - sent 12 octets to 
('10.0.1.255', 47808)

Thought about this more from perspective of migrating Alabaster's changelog, which is very much the type this ticket describes. I arbitrarily banged out minor and/or bugfix releases; the bugfix ones were actual bugfix-only releases, but the minor ones frequently had both bug-like and feature-like issues in them.

But, since it wasn't a Releases changelog, that data's not even there unless I retroactively add it. So they're all just bare list-items which Releases treats as implicit bug-type issues.

This means that after reformatting the file to a Releases style setup, only the bugfix/tertiary releases appear, with anything attached to minor releases falling into the "next feature release" bucket. Clearly not great; only way to fix without a lot of annotation grunt-work is to implement this feature here.

----

Re: that: I think the only real way to do it is:

* Add an option enabling "linear mode" or some other appropriate term
    * Probably, re: ticket OP, with another option toggling whether this behavior is limited to pre-1.0 releases or not.
        * I'd expect that to be on by default, since if you wanted your entire changelog to forever be this simple, why are you even using Releases? (But once this feature's implemented for pre-1.0, adding the toggle should be trivial, so why not.)
* When enabled, implicit/uncategorized line items would stop being classified as bugs, and instead lack *any* classification - probably both re: organizing *and* display.
    * This would, again, be closer to the conceptual model of "I'm not categorizing these at this point, they're all just issues". If one cares harder, one can manually annotate as usual, even interspersed with the implicit items if desired.
    * I still have to figure out the implementation/architectural details for how this would work, but pretty sure it's doable.

Hi @lockie,

Tornado-JSON is in fact designed to behave exactly this way and I will expound on why:
There are only two exception classes which return their log messages in failure responses; they are `APIError` (which `api_assert` raises), and `ValidationError` (which is raised when input/output validation fails on requests/responses). 

The reason this is this way is because these two exceptions have messages that are designed to be read by clients. An unhandled exception does not, and has the potential to leak internal data which you may not want the client to see.

Based on Tornado-JSON's philosophy at least, a good API should only ever raise `APIError` as a way of signalling to the client (with the appropriate HTTP Error Code); other unhandled exceptions are considered bugs in the server code and should be resolved or replaced with appropriate `APIError` assertions. The client should never be debugging the server :)

Now, if you're developing and in fact want to see all of these messages, you can [enable the *debug* setting](http://serverfault.com/questions/274014/how-to-set-debug-mode-in-tornado) which will allow you to see messages from unhandled exceptions as the `data` property in the response (which might look something like: `{"status": "failure, "message": "Internal Server Error", "data": "TypeError: blah blah blah", "code": 500}`). Of course, you don't want to enable this in a production API.

[Tornado-JSON's `write_error` method which handles this is defined here if you want to give it a quick look; the comment essentially states the same thing as I've said here.](https://github.com/hfaran/Tornado-JSON/blob/master/tornado_json/requesthandlers.py#L50)

> I'm not totally sold on the specific suggestion of doing it at the strategy level (it seems harder than some of the alternatives)

Yeah, it occurred to me that the only way I can think of implementing `.shared()` would be to generate all the examples ahead of time and return them encoded in the result of `shared`, since hypothesis can't control the order in which tests are run. This basically means keeping them in memory forever. Maybe that's okay for some use cases, but I guess a lot of people who have problems with the time it takes to generate objects are probably generating *big* objects.

> The easiest version is probably to give you an "always save examples which satisfy assumptions in the database" mode to run in.

I don't really know how the database works, but that sounds like it's probably a better idea.

> Firstly, tests written using Hypothesis are typically a bit longer than their corresponding quickcheck implementations. I'm not sure why but suspect some combo of Python testing culture, a tendency to more non-uniform interfaces to test, and it looking more natural in the language. So you might find it productive to combine some of your tests.

Yeah, I generally dislike the large size of typical unit tests in Python ;-)

> Secondly, in general strategies built up through the standard combinators shouldn't be too slow (this isn't really true in that there are a lot of things you can do that end up asking Hypothesis for huge data). So it's quite possible that you've just hit a performance bug.

Actually I think that most likely it's because the objects I'm building up are lots and lots of [pyrsistent](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyrsistent) objects, which are fairly fast but not as fast as built-in data structures, of course. I think that's probably a large part of why things are pretty slow.


> If you show me the strategy you're using (I think I saw an earlier version of it) I can take a look. The most likely culprit is that if you have nested collections you may wish to turn down the size of the interior values (either with an average_size or max_size parameter).

Here's the strategy:

https://gist.github.com/radix/1124c4c60e4616b791da

1. I realized while putting that gist together that the way I'm building locations is (still) dumb, since it can accidentally create a location with the same name twice. Since later they're put into a dict based on name, that means I'm basically doing some work that is going to overwritten if some location names clash.
2. I set average and max sizes for collections -- mostly just so the examples were tractable, since trying to debug my game states was becoming pretty difficult with the tons and tons of goop I was getting printed out :)
3. if you have any other feedback about improving those strategies I would massively appreciate hearing it.

Just as a little background: I'm not personally blocked by any performance issues with strategy generation; this kind of example-sharing just seemed like a good idea, since it seems like there's not a lot of benefit in generating different samples for different tests (at least in this kind of scenario). This code I'm working on is actually for a screencast I'm creating on Functional Programming in Python for O'Reilly, where the running example is a trivial text adventure game. So this isn't a serious app, just an educational example.

Hi David, it is not a trivial task to interpolate .fea text. 
- First it would need a some sort of object representation of all the data in the text, which in itself is difficult as it can only represent numerical data that relates directly to geometry. What to do with the rest what to do when masters have different values for non-interpolating fields? 
- Then suppose that all somehow works, instances can have more kerning pairs than each of the masters. So the stored position of the subtable breaks would be meaningless. 

I don't see a practical way of building this.

If you have really neat kerning lined up for each master (and perhaps even as a separate imports in the .fea) you could extract the numbers and use raw Mutator objects to calculate your values.

It might be useful if MutatorMath also respects the [info element](https://github.com/LettError/designSpaceDocument#32-info-element) in a master as an indication that this one needs to be the neutral/default/origin. If no master has this info element, then mutatorMath can use the method to pick one as does now.

One objection to this might be that there might be pre-variable-font designspaces in use in which the mutator-assigned default is not the master that has the info element set. Such systems might have different results. But I don't know how much bother this really would be. I don't want to introduce undocumented, unexpected results in systems that used to work.

Alternatively we can decide to define a separate attribute to the designspace source element that marks that source as the default.

I thought of a simpler "balanced" binary tree that I could write myself (not adding an external dependency), based on the fact that we know the rank order of each prediction at the beginning. The basic idea is that you know what the final balanced tree would look like, that is, which node would contain each number. So when you shuffle nodes around to rebalance, you can just always move them towards their final position.

Here's how it goes:

- For simplicity, suppose that the elements that we insert into the tree are the ranks, not the predictions themselves. Also assume `n` (list length) is a power of 2 for now.

- Assign each node a position in the final list: the root node is `n/2`, its left and right children are `n/4` and `3n/4` respectively, etc.

- When inserting a new value `x` at an occupied node `y`, we knock out `y` *if and only if* inserting it as a child of `x` would move it closer to its target/final position.

- Concretely, to add a new element `x` at a node that currently contains `y` (when the node's list is `i`):
    - If `x > y` and `y < i`, then replace `y` with `x` and try to insert `y` as the left child of `x`
    - If `x > y` and `y >= i`, then try to insert `x` as the right child of `y`
    - If `x < y` and `y > i`, then replace `y` with `x` and try to insert `y` as the right child of `x`
    - If `x < y` and `y <= i`, then try to insert `x` as the left child of `y`

- You can then easily get the rank of an element in O(log n) by keeping a counter of the number of children of each node.

I think this should allow a reasonably concise implementation (even when extending it to handle ties). But I don't know if it's worth the additional code complexity compared to a library dependency.

I could also just rewrite my stuff in Fortran and paste a license-compatible Fortran balanced tree implementation from somewhere else. Your call.

Well, I as a Windows user I expected these issues to be sorted out as part of the standard Python installation package, but they were not. It's a long standing issue. That's why I've put together this package.

It's the user who should require the Unicode functionality on his Windows Python installation. Why would a developer of a third party package want Unicode support explicitly for his package? Or rather I would think that every package developer implicitly expects that Unicode strings work – there is even not such a thing as a Unicode string in Python 3, it's just a string. It's not a matter of any particular third party package, it's a matter of whole Python installation.

Imagine I'm a developer of a simple Python package for creating and maintaining simple tree-like and tabular structures in plain text files. It may easily happen that a user has a foreign name in his table of names and when he tries to interactively display the table in his console on Windows, it raises an exception. Should every developer of every such a package be adding a dependency to `win-unicode-console` to his package? Should he be aware of any similar problem on any platform supported by Python and include a dependency to any other platform specific fix like that? Following this logic, even some packages from the standard Python library should be dependent on `win-unicode-console` since they may print a string originating from a user.

From the other side, imagine the following situation. A user installs a package that prints a string contaning non-ASCII characters and it results in an exception. The user asks the developers: your package raises this exception, is it a bug? The developers answer: it's not a bug in our package, it's a bug of Python itself – just try print("\u03b1") in your console and see. It's a long standing issue, `win_unicode_console` package tries to solve it – look there for more information. And that's it.

Actually after some time, people won't ask, they will just google the answer. This may increase the awareness of this problem among Python users and may show that there are many Windows Python users that want working Unicode in their consoles, so there will be greater pressure on Python core developers to fix the issue.

So, I just looked into this, and was able to replicate it. With further digging, I've come across the basic problem that Selectize and default Taggit have differing philosophies when it comes to supporting tags separated by either commas or spaces.

Selectize has a default delimiter setting of of a comma, in the `delimiter` setting. With taggit-selectize, we do not change this setting nor do we expose it to the user through our own settings. I think I'll change this.

Taggit, on the other hand, has a default string-to-tag parser that tries to be intelligent and parse tags separated by spaces if there are no quotes or commas in the string. So the problem here is that according to Selectize, the string "foo bar" is a single tag, so no commas or quotes are necessary. This then gets posted to Taggit, which says, "hey there, no commas, no quotes, this must be a space-separated tag list" and then pulls each word out separately as a distinct tag.

So the good news is that this is fixable, but really only by the user changing Taggit's default configuration.

Taggit has a setting that allows you to override the default tag parser, [documented here](https://django-taggit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/custom_tagging.html#using-a-custom-tag-string-parser). Their example creates a super naive parser that causes other problems with how Selectize works, so instead I have adapted a version of their tag parser that will still properly work with quotes and commas, but doesn't ever treat spaces as a separator.

I have included this function in `taggit_selectize.utils.parse_tags` and updated the README to reflect the extra step of configuring Taggit to use it during installation.

```
TAGGIT_TAGS_FROM_STRING = 'taggit_selectize.utils.parse_tags'
```

```
class PyTest(TestCommand):
```

Using `MXNDArrayGetData` to get the first element of the NDArray.

Notice: You should call `wait_to_read` before reading a NDArray and call `wait_to_write` before writing a NDArray.

There is no  `wait_to_write` in NDArray, so

`mx.nd.NDArray.wait_to_write = lambda self : _LIB.MXNDArrayWaitToWrite(self.handle)`

In case it helps anyone looking at this issue in the future - Treeherder (which is a ~SPA with a django-rest-framework API backend) has just switched to Auth0 and uses the following hybrid approach (for reasoning [see here](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1395356#c8)):
* the login redirect/callback is handled entirely by the SPA (using a public Auth0 client) - with the Auth0 user session being stored in localstorage / state synced between tabs / session renewal handled silently etc
* the callback page (and later renewals) also sends the Auth0 `access_token` and `id_token` to a django-rest-framework endpoint which calls `authenticate()` on a custom django auth backend
* this auth backend verifies the `id_token`, converts the `subject` and `email` into an appropriate Django username and then handles the rest of the Django login as one might expect, except the session expiry time is overridden to match that from the `access_token` / `id_token`

There are a few nice properties to this:
* the Auth0 access_token can be used client-side to regularly fetch short-lived Taskcluster tokens (required for interacting with third-party APIs), which are intentionally kept client-side only for security
* the custom django auth backend uses the same public Auth0 client for `id_token` verification, so doesn't need an Auth0 client secret - meaning that auth works out of the box in development (without the need for mocking or extra credentials to which contributors don't have access)
* the client side and django sessions now stay in sync (with the previous implementation one would expire before the other, leading to suboptimal UX where the user was told to log out and in again if they needed to perform client-side taskcluster actions)
* so long as the user visits Treeherder once every 24 hours, the silent renewal will keep both the Auth0 and django sessions alive indefinitely (or at least for several weeks I think? not sure if IAM have set a limit on silent renewals?)

For our implementation, see:
mozilla/treeherder#3144

There's probably a fair bit of overlap between our custom django auth backend and parts of this package - perhaps worth seeing if it's possible to reduce the duplication in the future? Though I imagine our use-case may be too specific to be of use to more straight-forwards SPA+django-rest-framework apps (who don't need to manage the Auth0 session client-side at all), so would understand if it's not worth the effort.

Oh, I see - the problem is that when your property raises an `AttributeError` then `AttrDict.__getattr__` is called and it will raise it's own `AttributeError` not mentioning the original (because it cannot).

This is actually a known Python behavior (you might even call it a bug) that currently has no workaround. The consensus amongst python people is to just make sure your properties don't raise `AttributeError` - that's what's causing `__getattr__` to be invoked.

Why this cannot be fixed is because there are legitimate use cases for this behavior - when raising `AttributeError` from a property should trigger a `__getattr__` call - it's an option left there for the programmer. If you don't wish it it's recommended you catch `AttributeError` and raise a different error instead, that will be correctly propagated.

As far as I know none of the properties defined by this library can ever raise `AttributeError` unless something goes seriously wrong (people messing with our objects' internal states etc). If you find a place where it is not so we will definitely treat it as a bug and fix it asap.

I just realized we have a really simple workaround, better than exposing a new global symbol (which might clash with existing symbols at module scope).

Instead of doing that, provide the `__qualname__` yourself for python2, and jsonpickle will pick that up.

```
class Foo(object):
    class Bar(object):
        __qualname__ = 'Foo.Bar'
```

Please try that out.  The only downside is that you might want to remove that line in the future if things ever move over to python3 fully.

jsonpickle itself will remain 2+3 compatible for the foreseeable future.

@Origilante, lemme see if I can answer your questions:

>  - Is there a way to know the size of the header from pgpy objects before the skedata.encrypt call? I'm working over PGPKey, and I need it to know how many bytes to encrypt to stay in the power-of-2 limit, because the first size is == len(header)+len(data)

This is something that I had to struggle with while getting packet creation working, certainly, because the length of the header can vary depending on the length of the rest of the packet. However, with partial body lengths, the length field in the header is always one octet long, so the packet header length should be static in this case.

The existing Header implementation does not yet support the Partial header length, but once it does, that should help solve that problem.

> so 10693-10681 == 12 bytes less, but the code looks the same as in PGPKey.encrypt. I can't figure where those 12 bytes are added, can you point me somewhere?

In the case where you let PGPKey do the heavy lifting for you, that is encrypting `plain_msg.__bytes__()` and when you do it manually you are encrypting only `MESSAGE`. So the second time you are not encrypting a packet header for a LiteralDataPacket, nor any of the additional data fields contained within it (a packet header, data format identifier, filename, and mtime). 

When I tested this difference with the message 'asdf' the difference was 8 bytes. I tried again with a much longer message (in this case, `MESSAGE = 'asdf' * 6144`) and got a difference of 12 bytes.

In the latter case, you have created an encrypted message that will probably not properly decrypt because it is no longer a valid OpenPGP message. If you change `ske.encrypt(sessionkey, cipher_algo, MESSAGE)` to `ske.encrypt(sessionkey, cipher_algo, plain_msg.__bytes__())` then the lengths will match up again :)

My proposal above would mean that many of the existing keywords would need to be deprecated. My proposed actions and reasoning is below. With term *loud deprecation* I mean emitting a deprecation warning right now and *silent deprecation* means documenting that keyword is deprecated but not yet emitting warning. Loudly deprecated keywords could then be removed already in 3.1, and silently deprecated keywords could be loudly deprecated at that point.

1. `Choose Ok/Cancel On Next Confirmation`: Totally useless keywords nowadays. Much better to pass the action to the keyword handling the alert as an argument directly. These should be deprecated loudly. Could even consider removing their underlying functionality altogether already at this point but that would obviously be backwards incompatible change.

2. `Confirm Action`: Can work as an alias for `Handle Alert` but should be silently deprecated.

3. `Get Alert Message`: This keyword currently dismisses the alert by default and can be configured to leave the alert in place with `dismiss=False`. In my opinion that is *really* strange behavior, and I'd like to change it so that alerts are accepted by default but it's possible to dismiss or leave them. The problem is that changing the default behavior would be backwards incompatible. Although I'd prefer an explicit keyword for getting the alert message, I don't think it's worth the effort deprecating the current behavior and changing it later. The proposed `Handle Alert` returns the message, and support everything this keyword should. Thus I think we should just silently deprecate this keyword in favor of `Handle Alert`.

4. `Dismiss Alert`: This keyword is totally messed up.. It currently *accepts* the alert by default and can be configured to leave it in place. There is actually no functionality to really *dismiss* the alert. In addition to that, the docs say that this keyword returns true/false when it actually returns the alert message. I would have preferred having separate explicit `Accept Alert` and `Dismiss Alert`, but that would have required a backwards incompatible change in this keyword. Probably easiest to just have 'Handle Alert` and deprecate this. Because this keyword currently works so strangely, I'd be willing to use loud deprecation.

5. `Input Text Into Prompt`:I know this particular alert is created by using `window.prompt()`, but for consistency with other alert keywords I'd propose renaming this to `Input Text Into Alert`. The old variant should be kept and silently deprecated.

I'm disinclined to accept this change for three reasons. 

The first is that these hanging indents rely on fixed width fonts and make the code look strangely indented with proportional width fonts. Text, including code, looks better in proportional width font, so I'd like not to lose support for that. 

Second, fonts aside, this form of indentation has limited editor support, often requiring the author to manually set and adjust the indentation, often over several lines. Some sophisticated editors can be configured to apply some form of this styling, but even then it often requires editor intervention.

Third, hanging indents often make code highly indented and strangely wrapped and sometimes necessarily lead to inconsistent forms. 

I've specifically been working to move away from this style, and these changes almost surely undo that work.

This is a crummy, subtle PYTHONPATH/sys.path issue.  I have been calling my tests from the root of a cloned git pip repo.  From the python docs at https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html,

> ``-``
  Read commands from standard input (sys.stdin). If standard input is a terminal, -i is implied.
  If this option is given, the first element of sys.argv will be "-" and the current directory will be added to the start of sys.path.

The key bit there is 

> the current directory will be added to the start of sys.path

So it doesn't matter how much virtualenv tries to put pip first.  My git clone is the front of sys.path, and when this happens in virtualenv.py::install_wheel:

```
    SCRIPT = textwrap.dedent("""
        import sys
        import pkgutil
        import tempfile
        import os

        import pip
```

It will currently always be importing pip from my git clone's pip folder.

So, I'm sorry for the noise.  The fix here isn't that the cacert.pem stuff needs to change, it's that we need a way to help ensure that virtualenv is using its bundled pip in preference to the front of sys.path here, or a better documented way of how to run tests to avoid this bad behavior.  Maybe this is all a moot point because it's a crappy artifact of how I'm trying to graft the newer vendored dependencies into the old folder structure - you all have already avoided this with the shift to the src folder.  I probably need to adapt more of master to 9.0.1 in order for this to work.

Anyway, thanks for humoring me.

```python
from streamparse import Spout
from pykafka import KafkaClient


class WordSpout(Spout):
    outputs = ['word']

    def initialize(self, stormconf, context):
        client = KafkaClient(hosts="c1:9092,c1_1:9092,c1_2:9092")
        topic = client.topics['test'.encode('utf-8')]
        self.balanced_consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
            consumer_group=b"test_group",
            auto_commit_enable=True,
            zookeeper_connect="c1:2181,c1_1:2181,c1_2:2181"
        )
        self.reg = self.regex()

    def next_tuple(self):
        message = self.balanced_consumer.consume()
        info = message.value.decode('utf-8')
        self.logger.info('==================={}'.format(info))
        word = next(self.words)
        self.emit([message])

```

Nice! Good call.

I'm not a big fan of storing the `StripeResponse` instance inside the `resp` dictionary however. I find it fairly confusing to have a dictionary that contains a `_last_response` key whose value is a `StripeResponse`, that has a `body` attribute whose value is the JSON string of the top-level dictionary, minus the `_last_response` key.

Instead, I think `StripeResponse` should contain both the deserialized dictionary and the JSON string, and `request()` should return a `StripeResponse` instead of a dictionary. This will require modifying a few places to look for the now nested dictionary (e.g. call `convert_to_stripe_object()` with `resp.data` instead of `resp`).

This would also bring the implementation closer to stripe-ruby's:
- https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby/blob/master/lib/stripe/stripe_response.rb
- https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby/blob/234b0809bfd737f7a8ddf215d23e15cab6efc741/lib/stripe/api_operations/create.rb#L5-L6

# returns a list of images and a list of labels for those images.
# the labels are the classification of each image (basically
# the directory name where it was stored)
def load_data(data_directory, file_extension):
    directories = [d for d in os.listdir(data_directory)
                   if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(data_directory, d))]
    labels = []
    images = []
    for d in directories:
        label_directory = os.path.join(data_directory, d)
        file_names = [os.path.join(label_directory, f)
                      for f in os.listdir(label_directory)
                      if f.endswith("." + file_extension)] 
        for f in file_names:
            images.append(skimage.data.imread(f))
            labels.append(int(d))
    return images, labels

Honestly, distributing binaries is always tricky. If you want to use a custom version of anything, you normally have to distribute it with the binaries, unless you can guarantee the user has the proper version installed at the correct path. The issue here is that once the libs are compiled, they're tied to the version of Python they compiled against. I suggest compiling different versions for different Python installations, or compiling against a shim that can handle redirecting the API calls to the appropriate version. Multiple versions is probably the simpler method. You could also just provide nupic via homebrew directly. That might be the most optimal solution. It would naturally be compiled against the homebrew version of Python.

Hi,

I was able to locate the problem and fix the error, but at the moment I can't reproduce all the steps I've performed to get it running. But I will try it.

Considering you are using also python3, the main problem is that if buildozer doesn't find a file called Kivy3.app it tries to download a dmg file from http://kivy.org/downloads/1.10.0/Kivy-1.10.0-osx-python3.dmg which obviously does not exist. Thus the downloaded file is invalid and hdiutil ist not able to attach it. And here, the long way of the journey to fix that problem begins :-D

The big hurdle to overcome is to generate your own Kivy3.app inside the temporary directory .buildozer/osx/platform/kivy-sdk-packager-master/osx using the script "create-osx-bundle.sh". Perform the following steps:

0. Download https://www.sveinbjorn.org/files/software/platypus.zip, copy it to your application folder and run it. Go to Playtypus->Preferences and click on "Install" to install the command line tools.

1. Install the requirements mentioned at https://kivy.org/docs/guide/packaging-osx.html#additional-libraries using homebrew and the commands
```
brew reinstall --build-bottle sdl2 sdl2_image sdl2_ttf sdl2_mixer
brew reinstall --build-bottle gstreamer gst-plugins-{base,good,bad,ugly}
pip3 install cython==0.25.2 # I use cython 0.25.2 instead of 0.26.1
```

2. Download GStreamer https://gstreamer.freedesktop.org/data/pkg/osx/1.14.0/gstreamer-1.0-1.14.0-x86_64.pkg and install it.

3. Download https://www.libsdl.org/release/SDL2-2.0.1.dmg and copy SDL2.framework to /Library/Frameworks
```
sudo cp -R /Volumes/SDL2/SDL2.framework /Library/Frameworks/
```
4. Download https://www.libsdl.org/projects/SDL_image/release/SDL2_image-2.0.0.dmg and copy SDL2_image.framework to /Library/Frameworks
```
sudo cp -R /Volumes/SDL2_image/SDL2_image.framework /Library/Frameworks/
```

5. Download https://www.libsdl.org/projects/SDL_mixer/release/SDL2_mixer-2.0.0.dmg and copy SDL2_mixer to /Library/Frameworks
```
sudo cp -R /Volumes/SDL2_mixer/SDL2_mixer.framework /Library/Frameworks/
```

6. Download https://www.libsdl.org/projects/SDL_ttf/release/SDL2_ttf-2.0.14.dmg and copy SDL2_tff.framework to /Library/Frameworks
```
sudo cp -R /Volumes/SDL2_ttf/SDL2_ttf.framework /Library/Frameworks/
```

7. To get the library libintl.8.dylib, install gettext via homebrew and create a symlink:
```
brew install gettext
sudo ln -s /usr/local/Cellar/gettext/0.19.8.1/lib/libintl.8.dylib /usr/local/lib/
```

8. Execute
```
cd .buildozer/osx/platform/kivy-sdk-packager-master/osx/
rm -Rf Kivy3.dmg
# To use python 3.6.2 instead of python 3.5.0

sed -i '' 's;3.5.0;3.6.2;g' create-osx-bundle.sh
./create-osx-bundle.sh python3
```
This step takes a while.

If you have problems with SDL2 then use this repository and replace the three SDL libraries in /Library/Frameworks:
https://github.com/inexorgame/tesseract/tree/master/src/xcode/Frameworks

9. Now you should have created your Kivy3.app file. Now go back to your project root and try to run `buildozer osx debug`. Maybe I'm mis-understanding the problem then. In the linked PR, they were evidently counting on `dayfirst` having no effect on *ambiguous* dates, or misunderstanding what the `dayfirst` parameter is intended to do. There *is* a persistent change in that regard, since it was a bug.

The changed behavior is that when you have a date like this: "2011-01-05", that can be parsed either as YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-DD-MM, and both of those are date formats that are actually in use, so the `dayfirst` parameter is used to specify that you want to prefer the YYYY-DD-MM format.

The issue I referred to above is for dates like 2011-01-15, which is completely unambiguous. In that case, the `dayfirst` and `yearfirst` parameters should be ignored, and it should parse to YYYY-MM-DD, but there is a bug that forces it to be interpreted as YYYY-DD-MM when `dayfirst` is specified, which raises an error.

Just remove the `dayfirst` parameter if you don't want to specify that the day is first.

This is behaving as expected per https://github.com/getsentry/responses/pull/171.

* Initially there is just one response registered.
* At the time of the first request is made, there is only one matching response so it is not discarded.
* When the second response is added, it is appended to the list of responses.
* When the second request is made, it iterates over the list and returns the first response again.  At this point the first response is discarded.
* Subsequent requests will return the second response.

Thank you for the suggestions. These are very good points. There are use cases where user want to trace both `boto3` and `httplib` as `boto3` tracing doesn't have as much granularity as `httplib` (retries, establishing connection etc). In that case although the service graph is not in an ideal shape due to lack of a mechanism to do custom aggregation, but trace timeline has much more information on the lowest python level information. 

But yes to your point the redundant nodes are confusing. I propose to have `patch_all` takes a flag of supporting indirect dependency or not. By default if library A and B are both supported but A depends on B, `patch_all()` will not patch B. But with a flag set to `True`, if will patch all libraries regardless of their relationships.

Do we want to keep the two distinct. Or would it be better to merge them?

> And other possible future plugins that cannot rely on system libs.

Then, these dependencies would have to be packaged first. The lookup done in `generate_candidate_libs` is not a desirable feature for Debian because it may override usage of the system libraries in favor of potentially unsafe ones. If a user do insist on using a custom version, he or she can explicitly do so by pointing `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to the desired location.

 > the freeimage plugin is the only one that makes use of this functionality.

That's one of such "unsafe" libraries. I have already patched a few CVEs for it on Debian (and I expect more to come in the future).

Based on your comments, I guess the only viable patching on my side is to prevent `load_lib` from using `generate_candidate_libs`.

> @benjaoming, can you tell us a bit about how that's been replaced in KA Lite, and how you approach things like what I described earlier in this thread:

@jamalex it was replaced with a debian package (`ka-lite-raspberry-pi`) that supports Nginx and depends on it. So everything is automatically setup. Even some extra settings for Nginx to optimize it.

https://github.com/learningequality/ka-lite-installers/blob/master/debian/debian/nginx.conf
+
https://github.com/learningequality/ka-lite-installers/blob/master/debian/debian/ka-lite.scripts-common#L439

> Would love to learn more about how you will be approaching managing the contents of these config files dynamically. There are several values in the nginx config that depend on internal mutable Kolibri options

The Debian pkg can ship with a default configuration for the system user `kolibri`. It is intended that some files are placed on the system as "read only", and others are there for the user to adjust. Critical is to not invite users to change files that are managed by dpkg, as it will give warnings during upgrades, where users have to merge changes manually (or select between local version and package maintainers version).

I think we could consider a `settings.ini.dist` kind of pattern, whereby if `settings.ini` doesn't exist then the package will copy from `settings.ini.dist`. I'm not sure what non-default settings would be essential for the system yet.

For the Nginx configuration, we need to define `STATIC_ROOT`, `MEDIA_ROOT` etc. to be served in the right locations -- but only for the default setup. If users change it, they would have to adjust their Nginx configuration as well.

Since you appear to know the location of your Metadata Server, let's start from there:

```
proc iomoperate
    uri='iom://metadataserver:8561;Bridge;USER=my_user,PASS=my_pass';
    list defined servers;
quit;
```

this should give you the information you need about the Object Spawner, output in the log should be something like the following:

```
    Object Spawner - myhostname (A5V7DU9J.AY000003)
        Server class : IOM Spawner
        Spawnable server component : SASApp - Stored Process Server
        Spawnable server component : SASApp - Workspace Server
        Operator port : 8581
        PortBank port : 8801
        PortBank port : 8811
        PortBank port : 8821
        Machine name : host.domain.com
```

You want the attribute 'Machine name' as your IOM host address for SASPy configuration

Also from the output of the above you should get information for you Workspace server(s), which will also be listed above as a 'Spawnable server component'

```
    SASApp - Workspace Server (A5V7DU9J.AY000005)
        Server class : 440196D4-90F0-11D0-9F41-00A024BB830C
        Logical name : SASApp - Logical Workspace Server
        Server context : SASApp
        Bridge port : 8591
        Machine name : host.domain.com
```

You want the attribute 'Bridge Port' as your IOM port number for SASPy configuration

Going to respond in one comment and quote the relevant parts.

> Why would Anaconda be different from Miniconda?

This is based on my understanding of the two and their different audiences: Anaconda is bootstrap Conda and populate it with the entire scientific Python stack.  The goal of downloading Anaconda is to have everything ready-to-use.  Miniconda is meant when you need to bootstrap Conda onto a system without Python or where you can't write to the system Python using something like `pip install conda`.

The only code other than Conda that ships with Miniconda is Python and that's only shipped because Conda needs Python to run.  We could create a default environment with just Python in it.  I don't see any problem in that, but for the cases where I've used Miniconda (mostly Wakari) I just wanted the ability to run `conda` commands to create the environments that I needed.

> I think you are conflating two issues here, which is quite confusing, as it (incorrectly) makes it sound like they are both required to fix all the issues you bring up. One issue is putting conda in a separate environment from the default one (#1022), and one is changing how conda resolves the root environment location (#1093).

You're correct in identifying those as separate issues.  They are and they can both be solved separately.  Where I think they're connected is that conda has to be installed into a root environment because it uses `sys.prefix` to resolve its location.  That's what gives rise to the concept of a system environment (open to a better name to avoid overloading the term environment).

I should probably make it more clear.  Let me try restating and see if this makes more sense:

---

Anaconda and Miniconda should create a so-called system environment that is always on the PATH.  Ideally, for backwards compatibility, this would default to what is currently called the root environment.  Things should not be allowed to install into that environment unless they have a `type` of `system` (for example, conda, conda-build, conda-env) or the user specifically overrides that behavior.

For pip-based installations, we don't worry about this system environment, though we expose the mechanism for creating that environment via a command like: `conda create --system` which would move conda over to that and walk the user through updating their PATH appropriately.  It should be noted that `conda update --system conda` should not work in this type of deployment without converting to a more standard conda-like environment.

---

Does that make it more clear or less?

My goal is to take Conda out of the environments and such that it manages.  Once we make that a separate concern, then conda is focused on manipulating files that exist in a specified place that's addressed by: 1) environment variable, 2) configuration; 3) convention.  This makes it easier to ensure that the conda system I'm looking at from Node.js is the same one that Python sees.  Otherwise, I have to ask the Python version.

Combining these two issues is to focus on the benefits of that goal rather than focusing on a fix for one.

---

As I got to this point the rest of the comments came in:

> 2. What would be the resulting line in bashrc with this proposal?


This depends on where we end up with code located.  Let's assume `~/.conda` becomes the default location for everything and that's where Anaconda and Miniconda initialize themselves.

```bash
export PATH=$HOME/.conda/bin:$PATH
source activate $(conda config --get core.default_env)
```

> 3. Regarding question 2, what about Windows?

I would have to figure this out.  Something like `anaconda.bat` would make it trivial, but I'm not sure what our options are doing startup script style initialization on Windows.

> 4. Can you explain what your conception of conda init is? I feel like your conception of it is different from what it currently does.

Yup -- sorry I didn't call that out.  When I talk about `conda init`, I mean what I want it to do, not what it does.  So here's what I'm thinking:

* Determines if the root directory exists along with some signifier that it's been initialized
* If so, displays an error saying its already there and stop
* If not, creates that directory and the envs and pkgs directories and touches the `.initialized` or what have you file.

Done.  Now `conda install` would use that as the basis for finding local packages, storing environments, and so on.

To reiterate, at this point the `conda` package can exist anywhere.  There wouldn't be any bootstrapping of new packages or anything like that that we need to do as that newly minted conda system/environment/install is now setup to be worked on from the outside.  This feeds into question 5…

> 5. In a similar vein, I'm unclear how this affects pip install conda. Note that the main fundamental issue with pip install conda is that the Python is not managed by conda (which is basically by definition).

Correct.  That said, conda simply needs Python to run and can be run using any Python as far as I'm aware.

I wasn't involved in the development process, so please correct me if I'm wrong, but it appears that the main motivation here was that conda grew out of Anaconda.  Anaconda's main goal is to be ready-to-use.  As such, Python has to be on the path when you start.  As far as I'm aware, there are no technical reasons that Ubuntu's `python` package can't be the Python that executes the `conda` command other than the reliance on `sys.prefix`.

> Some of your activate issues could be solved by #813 …

I don't see how this is directly relevant.  It addresses one of the concerns, but not the overarching goals of removing the root environment from the equation entirely.

> 7. If you were going to completely reimplement conda in another language, why would you even need to access conda at all? If this were truly done, Python would not even be needed.

Currently there is no way to interoperate with a Python-based installation of conda from the outside.  I'm running into this in my work with Anaconda Launcher where I'm implementing a read-only version of conda to handle gathering information about what's installed.  I can't know where to start without using `conda info` because the location of the conda installation depends entirely on where it was installed.

This is an issue for ecosystems that want to re-implement the conda spec in their own language but want to play nicely with other installations of conda that might be managed by Python.  @pzwang might have some thoughts on this as I know he's talked to other communities that love conda as a concept but will not install Python to manage packages.

> 8. With this proposal, what files would be in the ~/anaconda directory (say, with a default installation)?

Here's an example structure of what I think should be in a conda installation:

```
./.conda-initialized <-- denotes an initialized install
./envs/ <-- environments directory
./pkgs/ <-- contains the local packages
```

For an environment with the a system environment as well, you would drop the appropriate directories needed for that (`bin`, `lib`, `share`, and so on).  That said, their location is entirely independent of the conda installation it operates on so you could just as easily install it into `/usr/local` or `~`.

> 9. Wait, what exactly does the --system flag do? Does the "system level" mean, e.g., /usr/? If so, -100. If not, that's a bad name.

Consider it a short-hand for `conda update --prefix=~/anaconda conda` (assuming the default location for Miniconda/Anaconda was used).  I'm definitely open for a name, but there needs to be a short-hand for updating the main prefix that contains conda and the related packages that are always available.

For a self-managed conda (something installed via Miniconda/Anaconda and not via pip or rpm or any other package manager), it would be a short-hand to the system environment: the environment that contains conda.  It should be an environment that's always on the PATH (if configured by the user -- if they didn't add it it should never be on the PATH) and should not be activate-able.  For pip-based installations (i.e., one where `$(which conda)/../conda-meta` does not exist), it would provide an error and give the user an error message that prompts them to create a conda-managed installation (`conda init --system`, for example).

> 10. Accordingly, I don't understand what the system type means. It makes a package uninstallable??

This is actually going to end up being two-fold:

* Environments would have an allowable type of package that can be installed into it (i.e., everything, system, and so on)
* When installing a package into an environment, the package type would need to be allowed by that environment or the user would have to explicitly state that they want to ignore that.

The reason is to avoid this:

```bash
$ source activate dev
$ conda install conda
```

And instead require:

```bash
$ source activate dev
$ conda install conda --override
```

At that point they're saying they want that installed for sure into that environment and are willing to go against the wishes of the package maintainer and/or really know what you're doing.

> If you have two packages that have colliding directories and files, conda doesn't handle the problem -- it just crashes. If you can handle this problem, then the bug i reported will be handled nicely as well.

This will be fixed as part of https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/3571 in 4.3.

A few changes have been made in the past few months:

- `conda update --all` completely ignores the `anaconda` metapackage. Since `anaconda` almost always prevents updates, it makes little sense to include it. As noted, `conda update anaconda` and `conda update --all` are mutually exclusive ways of keeping things up-to-date.

- `conda update --all` no longer requires that each package that is installed be updated only. That is, it can downgrade a package, if it leads to the overall satisfiability of packages. Since conda already tries to make everything as up-to-date as possible, this results in far fewer unsatisfiability issues, which were usually caused because one package's dependency specifications required another to be downgraded.

- The unsatisfiable package specification hint generation is now very fast, even for a large number of packages.  It no longer hangs or bails with no hint. 

- The algorithm can still be a little slow. Not many changes have been made in the past six months there (one memory leak in Python 2 has been fixed recently). It should generally finish, though, unless you run out of memory, or are too impatient. For `conda update --all`, it shouldn't take more than a minute at the absolute worst on a reasonably fast machine. 

- A few instances of over specified dependencies in the Anaconda metadata have been fixed.

> The recommended way to install conda on Linux is and will remain installing Anaconda or Miniconda.

Running some arbitrary shell script via “download shell script and run it” is – quite rightly – deprecated as a security flaw; we expect users to be very wary of such advice. Much better to have `conda` installable using the operating system package manager.

```python
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch
import logging

# My app logger
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
my_log_level = logging.DEBUG
logging.basicConfig(level=my_log_level)
logger.debug("Init app")

# print("Current loggers:", logging.Logger.manager.loggerDict.keys())

# Elasticsearch logger to be silenced
loggerES = logging.getLogger('elasticsearch')
loggerES.setLevel(logging.CRITICAL)
loggerUrlib = logging.getLogger('urllib3')
loggerUrlib.setLevel(logging.CRITICAL)

# Test a failing connection
es = Elasticsearch([{'host': 'doesnotexist'}], timeout=2)
try:
    es.ping()
    logger.info("Connected")
except:
    logger.info("Failed")

logger.debug("End of app")
```

You can add properties to a subdictionary if it is a dictionary, but the root object is rarely (ever?) a dictionary.

@sethdeckard you can achieve this by combining the root container with non-root containers.
e.g.
```
$root/index.html
assets/style.css
assets/main.js
assets/sub_dir/example.ts
```
For example, use $root for your index file and have an 'assets' container then you can batch upload to that assets container. If you need more subdirectories under assets, the files in that container *can* use slashes ('sub_dir/example.ts' would be the file name for example).

I found the answer that solved my problem as well. I just document it a bit more properly here:

The solution is just to initialize the client session using:

    import socket # together with your other imports

    conn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(
            family=socket.AF_INET,
            verify_ssl=False,
        )
        
       # Create client session that will ensure we dont open new connection
       # per each request.
       async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=conn) as session:

Explanation based on the link:
The client session will use the connection instead of the default AsyncResolver as the resolver for the connection. It used to be the default resolver. The problem seems to be related to domains with ipv6 where the AsyncResolver has problems, so the solution is to simply specify the family to ipv4 addresses which is what we do with `family=socket.AF_INET`.

Depends on how lite. For the R kernel, I don't relish the idea of trying to integrate an HTTP server event loop with the loop listening on the ZMQ sockets (which currently just uses zmq poll).

> > And then it needs to think about what to do if it doesn't get that receipt within a timeout, and so on.
>
> That's a small detail, which I'd hope the asynchronous pattern of choice could handle well.

I feel like we're talking at cross purposes here. I know it could all be solved with enough async cleverness. My point is that if you don't have this message asking the frontend to load a specific resource, you avoid the whole problem entirely, and the kernel code doesn't need to do clever async stuff.

By analogy, when you request a web page, it contains references to images, JS etc. that the page requires. It doesn't try to tell the frontend to load those resources before loading the page*: the frontend parses the page and requests the resources it needs.

(* OK, so HTTP 2 will in fact do some of this as an optimisation. But I imagine that all the abstractions for servers and frontends will continue to make it look like it's the frontend requesting resources, not the backend pushing them)

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

The `yara.Rules` object is not a pure-Python object, it's implemented via a C extension and it doesn't support pickling. Instead of passing the compiled rules to your workers you could launch the workers first, passing the rules in text form, and compile the rules in the `work` function. In other words, instead of compiling the rules in the main process, each worker is responsible of compiling the rules for themselves.

I mainly meant it as an academic question: how small can we make the FLOP cost for a reverse-mode implementation of GGN-vector products (or even for JVPs), compared to a mixed-mode solution? That's interesting for our understanding of autodiff, and also more relevant to people who are using systems without full mixed-mode capabilities.

It might be relevant for TensorFlow, where graph manipulation overheads don't matter so much because they only happen ahead-of-time. Wouldn't this let us generate FLOP-efficient JVP routines in TF, by calling tf.gradients twice? (That might insert extra ops in the TF graph that aren't necessary, but in principle it should generate code that is FLOP-efficient, I think.)

```
from packaging.version import LegacyVersion
from skbuild.exceptions import SKBuildError
from skbuild.cmaker import get_cmake_version

# Add CMake as a build requirement if cmake is not installed or is too low a version
setup_requires = []
try:
    if LegacyVersion(get_cmake_version()) < LegacyVersion("3.4"):
        setup_requires.append('cmake')
except SKBuildError:
    setup_requires.append('cmake')
```

Note also that instead of appending `cmake`, you  could specify `cmake>=X.Y.Z`

that said, if installing the cmake wheel is not a problem, it is probably easier to do the following:

```
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools", "wheel", "cmake>=X.Y.Z"]
```

@danwalmsley - 
We have since improved that error to:
PackageTargetFallback is deprecated. Replace PackageTargetFallback references with AssetTargetFallback in the project environment.

More details...PackageTargetFallback worked like imports did in Project.json.
The teams have realized that our implementation of that wasn't ideal.
In order to not break old projects/packages -- we created AssetTargetFallback which is implicitly set for netcoreapp/netstandard2.0 projects via targets.
Some project templates, in the past, used PackageTargetFallback.
When upgrading those projects to v2.0, you end up with both PTF and ATF in effect. Thus we error.
Ideally, you just move to ATF from your existing PTF.

- `/root/of/source/tree`
- `/root/of/source/tree/my/handlers/package`

And, again IIUC, this means that sys.modules can easily end up with two imports of the same package but at different paths. Do you concur?

@NikolajBjorner: let me follow your example and use a mathematically precise language to concisely state our problem:

Problem description
-------------------------
- There is a quantifier-free background (base) CSP y, which is known to be sat. (Since y may contain nonlinearities, we cannot rule out the possibility that the SMT-solver returns unknown, but that is a different story.)
- All I variables x1, ..., xI in y have finite domains D1, ..., DI. The necessary domain constraints are part of the background CSP y.

- For each variable xi there is a finite sequence of Ji finite subdomains Sij.
- For each i = 1, ..., I and each j = 1, ..., Ji the subdomain Sij is a subset of the domain Di.
- For each i the Ji subdomains Sij are disjoint.
- For each i and j there is a domain constraint cij, which restricts variable xi to the subdomain Sij.

For simplicity, we assume that each of the domain constraints cij individually is compatible with the background CSP y, i.e. the CSP obtained by adding a single cij to y is still sat.

- We wish to obtain k = 1, ..., K solutions (models) Mk.
- For all i and j there shall be at least one solution Mk, in which variable xi has a value from subdomain Sij.
- The number K of solutions shall be minimized.

Clearly, for the number K of solutions we have the inequalities K >= Ji for all i, since the Sij are disjoint for fixed i.

If a mathematically strict minimum for K were too costly to compute, we would be satisfied with an approximate minimum.

Heuristic algorithm (A)
----------------------------
Our approximate, heuristic algorithm (A) described in the earlier comments works like this:

```
;; initialization phase, for now outside the solver
for i = 1 to I
  put all constraints cij into a FIFO queue Ci
endfor

enter y to the solver
check-sat ; we are at context stack level 0 and have a base solution for y
push the context ; preserve the solver's internal sat state

;; computation phase
while there is a variable xi with non-empty queue Ci
  for i = 1 to I 
    if Qi is non-empty
       pop a domain constraint cij from queue Ci
	   assert cij
	   check-sat
       if sat
	     push the context ; preserve the solver's internal sat state
	   else
	     pop the context ; discard the solver's internal unsat state
	     add cij at the end of Ci
	   endif
    endif
  endfor
  get-model and save it externally
  pop sufficiently often so that we are back at context stack level 0 where we have preserved our base sat context for y
endwhile
```

In effect, each variable gets one chance to acquire a value from a subdomain Sij via the domain constraint cij. If cij is incompatible with the domain constraints previously issued in the current round, the variable xi is left free. Its value will be chosen by the solver.

The push and pop machinery working on the solver's internal context stack in our experience enormously improves effiency.

Several variations/improvements are conceivable:

The algorithm (A) sketched above simply uses the numbering of the variables, and it ignores the size of the queues. A probable improvement would be to use a priority queue for X, and to sort the variables according to the size of the associated constraint queues Ci.

Heuristic algorithm (B)
----------------------------
In heuristic algorithm (B) the computation phase would be altered into:

```
form a variable priority queue X which initially contains all variables xi with a non-empty constraint queue Ci ; all variables in our case
sort the variables xi in X according to the size of the associated constraint queue Ci, in descending order
while the variable queue X is non-empty
  pop a variable xi from the variable queue X 
  pop a domain constraint cij from the constraint queue Ci
  assert cij
  check-sat
  if sat
    push the context onto the context stack ; for later use
  else
    pop the context stack ; the context has become useless
    add cij at the end of Ci
  endif
  if the variable queue X is empty
     get-model and save it externally
     pop the context stack sufficiently often so that we are back at context stack level 0 where we have preserved our base sat context for y
  	 add all variables xi with non-empty constraint queue Ci back into variable queue X
  endif
endwhile
```

Clearly, other algorithms for sorting the variable queue X come to mind, such as most constrained first, etc. Such a sort algorithm would require an analyze of the constraints in the base CSP y. If the algorithm ran completely internally inside the solver, the solver would presumabmly employ its own prioritization strategy for the variables.

The next improvement that comes to mind is to not assert the remaining domain constraints cij
one at a time. Instead, when variable xi is considered, the disjunction of the remaining domain constraints cij is asserted. This gives the solver more latitude to satisfy one of the domain constraints cij.

Heuristic algorithm (C)
----------------------------
In heuristic algorithm (C), instead of constraint queues Ci, we would use contraint sets Ci. The computation part would read:

```
form a variable queue X which contains all variables xi with a non-empty constraint set Ci
sort the variables xi in X according to the size of the associated constraint set Ci, in descending order
while the variable queue X is non-empty
  pop a variable xi from the variable queue X 
  assert the disjunction of all constraints cij in Ci, i.e. (or ci1 ci2 ...)
  check-sat
  if sat
    push the context onto the context stack ; for later use
	find out which of the mutually exclusive domain constraints cij in Ci was satisfied, and remove it from Ci
  else
    pop the context stack ; the context has become useless
  endif
  if the variable queue X is empty
     get-model and save it externally
     pop the context stack sufficiently often so that we arrive at context stack level 0 where we have preserved our base sat state for y
  	 add all variables xi with non-empty constraint set Ci back into variable queue X, and sort the latter
  endif
endwhile
```

Concurrency
----------------
There is room for concurrency. 

Let us assert the disjunctions Di = (or ci1 ci2 ...) upfront directly after defining all variables, but before asserting any constraints from the background CSP y.

```
...
(assert (or c11 c12 ... ))
(assert (or c21 c22 ... ))
(assert (or c31 c32 ... ))
...
```

Then the strategy

`(check-sat-using (par-do split-clause smt))`

would solve [C1| dressed CSPs in parallel. Here I have used the hypothetical tactical par-do (https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/479) that first applies the tactic split-clause to the original goal, and then the tactic smt concurrently to each of the subgoals produced by split-clause.

The tactical `par-do` should have a parameter, say `:max-workers`, by which one could limit the number of threads concurrently working on the subgoals. So we would issue something like

`(check-sat-using ((! par-do :max-workers 6) split-clause smt))`

which should work fine on mylaptop which is sporting 8 cores. (If, behind the scenes, Z3 supported truly distributed computing, one could use a much higher number of workers, of course.)

A problem with the strategy above is that a solution for y would be rediscovered over and over again by the different threads.

So my questions: 

1. Assuming that there were a tactical `par-do`, is there a way, using tactics and tacticals, to compute a base solution for the naked CSP y that could be re-used (via the internal context stack) when concurrently computing the dressed solutions for y plus some extra domain constraints cij?
2. How much of the algorithms above are amenable to be encoded into tactics and tacticals (including, if necessary, new hypothetical ones)?

I stop with this questions and will address the desired optimization in another comment.

Side remark
---------------
A side remark on the types we use, which is not directly relevant to the specification of the general problem:
- The variables have native types (sorts) Bool, Int, Real or String.
- Some Int variables have a 0-1 domain. We work with pseudo-booelan functions.
- All Real variables have decimal types, which is enforced by special constraints on each variable.
- (If there were subtypes in the SMT-LIB 2 world, we could define the 0-1 variables and the decimal veriables elegantly using subtypes.)
- We would like to work with String variables with regexp-xonstraints, but do not yet know how to do this within Z3.

The number Ji of subdomains typically varies from 1 to 15.
I suppose it could potentially work if we use -custom on one part of the library and not use it on the other parts. I dimly remember that not being supported either though.

regarding the second question: the models it can find have soundG_w set to "true". By asserting it to false, the previous model cannot be used (and the formula may be unsat). Z3 is not able to either establish unsat or find a model in these cases.

The issue with keeping nolearn in sync with Lasagne is that the latter 
introduces breaking changes in Git (and that's ok).  So what I do is I 
say: this version of nolearn is known to work with this Git version of 
Lasagne (through requirements.txt).  If you want to use a newer version 
of Lasagne then you're on your own.  (No way I'll guarantee that every 
latest commit in Git doesn't break things.)  This will be more relaxed 
once there's Lasagne releases and maybe a more conservative deprecation 
policy.

@glasserc 
> Is there an option for ujson to let us not serialize bytes, but only the usual booleans, integers, floats, strings, lists, dicts, and None?

No, there isn't : str and bytes are handled [the same way](https://github.com/esnme/ultrajson/blob/master/python/objToJSON.c#L543) - lines 543,550.
But ujson will raise on non utf8 bytes, though, so it's safe-ish.

To my mind, what should be enforced is consistency between memory and postgresql storage, by calling `record = ujson.loads(ujson.dumps(record))` before storing in the memory backed (in `memory.Storage.create` and `memory.Storage.update`.
It would have caught this issue and would also catch non-dict objects, etc.

If lambdas are supported by your compiler, you could change:
`depth_sensor.start(*rgb);`
to something like:
`depth_sensor.start([&](){ rgb->operator(); });
If lambdas are unavailable, you could create a wrapper class that takes your base class by reference and issues a virtual call in its `operator()`.

## GraphQLField with name

This PR is also adding the `name` into the `GraphQLField` definition.
We had this one year ago and having the field to hold it's name leaked some logic into the wrong places.

### Explanation
In the js reference implementation, there are two type definitions: `GraphQLField` and `GraphQLFieldDefinition` (one to be the "compiled" version of the other).
This complicates things unnecessarily. It's much easier to hold the field name information into the field map itself, and have it as source of truth for field naming.
So this implementation diverges a bit from the reference on that.

Some interesting commits about this decision: 
* Adding field definitions: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/commit/2b0232f611f46a3ffdb8315be0fff3f321b2a361
* Simplifying field definitions (after): https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/commit/6519b7e708bd9a447ca47ede055e1c8048fe4705#diff-881aeb1155f157c09b51a740b51fabe4

There is a good reason to have the extra paragraph in the one file which clarifies legal aspects: The 3-clause BSD license does not clarify the status of pull requests on GitHub or e.g. patches sent via email, which can put larger project with many external contributions into an awkward situation. An alternative would be to use CLAs, which are annoying. (Quite frankly, I think the discussion of whether ``LICENSE.txt`` has the MD5 sum of the standard 3-clause BSD file a bit silly.) In this case, it is a perfectly reasonable (and readable) addition that does not encumber the software in any way, and which just clarifies interactions with other users.

PS: Try googling for this specific extra paragraph -- you will find that many other projects use it.

If I `change' it to 9600 with stty, the noise mavproxy reads from the remains characteristically the same; if I then use stty to set it back to 57600, then mavproxy stops seeing noise and starts seeing the expected valid MAVLink stream.

Wrt setting the system time. What do you mean?
- Updating the drones time? Not sure that is possible...
- Updating the GCS time? Not sure that is wanted...

Currently what I do is run a sync every second and keep the following:
time_send
time_receive

As a result the delta between GCS time and drone time is:
dtime = (time_send + time_receive) / 2 - drone_time;

And when doing that a lot the dtime will average to the correct delta. As a result it is now possible to know exactly when a "GLOBAL_POSITION_INT" was created in GCS time.

I can add a function that would report back GCS time, if you give it a drone time ... (in a new PR)
